segments.s512-g41: به رفتن is a prepositional phrase 'regarding/to going'; چنو (archaic/chauhn) = 'so/thus' and گشت is a past-tense verb 'became' (3rd-sg) forming the predicate; همداستان is the predicate adjective 'of one mind, agreed.' Together this clause means 'he became agreed/ resolved about going.' Word order is poetic: the verb گشت follows the adverbial and adjective.
segments.s512-g42: فرستید is a perfect/past 3rd-person plural 'they sent' (used here to report an action); با او 'with him' marks accompaniment; به هندوستان marks the destination with به. The object of فرستید is not stated in the clause (omitted and recoverable from context), and the clause functions as a result: 'they sent (someone) with him to India.'
segments.s512-g43: من is the first‑person subject 'I'; ایدر (archaic) = 'here'; همه کار کردم is a complete clause 'I did all work' with کردم first‑person past. به برگ is a short prepositional phrase (به + برگ, literally 'for/to a leaf/page'); in poetic usage it serves as a terse adverbial phrase attached to the clause (here the exact idiomatic force is emphatic/brief — 'for a mere leaf' or 'for a page' as a small reward), hence placed after the verb for metrical/poetic order.
segments.s512-g44: به بیچارگی is a prepositional phrase 'in/with misery'; دل نهادم is literal 'I placed the heart' (نهادم = I put/placed, past 1st of نهادن) — an idiom meaning 'I resolved' or 'I set my heart'; به مرگ 'toward death' (به + مرگ) indicates the object/goal of that resolve: 'I set my heart on death.'
segments.s512-g45: نخست 'first' (adverb); آنک is an archaic connective/relative 'that/so that' introducing what ought to be done; تابوت زرین is the noun phrase 'golden coffin' (تابوت + adjective زرین); کنند is 3rd-person plural present/subjunctive used optatively/imperatively here ('let them make'/'they should make'). The clause is a wish/command about funerary preparations.
segments.s512-g46: کفن is the direct object 'shroud'; بر + تنم = 'upon my body' (بر = on/upon, تنم = 'my body' with enclitic -م). عنبر آگین is a compound: عنبر (ambergris/amber) + آگین 'adorned/imbued with' — together 'scented/adorned with amber(gris).' کنند again is the optative 'let them do/let them make.' The clause orders that the body be wrapped in an amber‑scented shroud.
segments.s512-g47: ز is the poetic preposition 'of/from' (introducing the material); زربفت چینی is a compound noun 'Chinese gold-brocade' (زربفت = gold-brocade, چینی = Chinese); سزاوار من is an adjectival phrase 'worthy/fit for me' (سزاوار + possessive من) that follows the noun in inverted poetic order: 'Chinese gold-brocade worthy of me.'
segments.s512-g48: کسی is an indefinite 'someone/anyone'; کو here is an archaic particle (classical usage) connected with question/indefinites and does not mean modern 'where' (its precise force is idiomatic and marks the interrogative/indefinite construction); بپیچد is the present/subjunctive 3rd‑sg of پیچیدن 'to wrap/turn' used optatively ('who will wrap'); ز تیمار من is a prepositional phrase 'out of my care/solicitude' — taken together: 'Is there anyone who will wrap (me) in [that cloth] out of care for me?'
segments.s512-g33: Tu (تو) is the explicit subject 'you'. The phrase فرزند خوانش functions as a compound meaning 'his adopted son' (خوانش here is an archaic/poetic form related to 'خوانده‌اش' — 'his foster/adopted (one)'). The particle نه is a negator that contrasts the following predicate 'داماد من' ('my son‑in‑law'); the whole clause reads as a contrastive predication: 'you are his adopted son, not my son‑in‑law.' Word order is Subject — predicate nominal with the negation placed before the contrasted predicate.
segments.s512-g34: بدو is a dative/adverbial 'to him' (be + او), not the imperative 'run'; تازه کن is an imperative construction ('make fresh / renew') where تازه is the adjective 'new/fresh' and کن is the verb 'do/make' in the imperative. در جهان is a locative phrase 'in the world', and یاد من is the object 'my remembrance / my name (memory of me)'. The word order is flexible (dative + verb + locative + object), giving the sense 'make my remembrance new for him in the world.'
segments.s512-g35: دگر modifies دختر as 'the other/another daughter.' کید here functions appositionally as a proper name or identifier of that daughter (reading 'the daughter Kēd'). را is the definite/object marker that marks this noun phrase as the direct object of the following verb. بی‌گزند is an adverbial/predicative phrase 'unharmed / safe' describing the condition in which she should be sent.
segments.s512-g36: فرستید here is a past narrative form 'they sent / (were) sent' (classical past), taking the preceding را‑marked NP as its object; in some contexts it can also be read as an imperative, but narrative past fits the surrounding lines. نزد is a prepositional/relational word meaning 'to / in the presence of' (i.e. 'to the care/presence of'). پدر ارجمند is the head noun 'father' with the postpositive adjective 'ارجمند' ('esteemed / venerable').
segments.s512-g37: This is a coordinative list of companions/people. ابا appears as an item (here treated as a name or label in context); یاره is 'companion / follower'; برده is 'slave' (or 'one taken'); نیک‌خواه is 'well‑wisher.' The conjunction و links the items. The whole phrase names those who accompany or are gathered.
segments.s512-g38: عمار is the agent/name 'Ammar.' بسیچید is a past tense verb 'mustering/gathered' (from بسیجیدن), indicating he marshaled forces or people. بااو is a composite prepositional phrase 'with him' (با + او). به راه is another adverbial phrase meaning 'on the road / on the march.' Together they express 'Ammar mustered (them) and, with him, set out on the way.'
segments.s512-g39: همان is a demonstrative 'the same / that very.' It prefaces a list of valuables: افسر (a helmet or headpiece), گوهر (a gem/jewel), سیم (silver — omitted from the word list but present in the string), and زر (gold). This is a simple noun phrase enumerating items previously mentioned or brought.
segments.s512-g40: که introduces a relative clause 'which/that.' آورده is the past participle 'brought' and بود functions here as the auxiliary forming the past perfect / pluperfect ('had brought'). او is the subject pronoun 'he.' ز پیش پدر is a prepositional phrase 'from before (or from the presence of) his father' meaning 'from his father / from his father's house' (ز 'from' + پیش 'before / in the presence of' + پدر 'father'). The clause modifies the listed items: 'which he had brought from his father.'
segments.s512-g17: This is a relative clause introduced by the fused word هرانکس (literally 'whoever'). که بودند is a past plural copula ('who were') and ز ایرانیان means 'from/among the Iranians' (ز is the poetic preposition 'from/among'). Word order is typical classical Persian: relative pronoun + verb + origin phrase.
segments.s512-g18: کزیشان is a compound relative (کز + ایشان) meaning 'of whom' or 'whose' and links back to 'ایرانیان'. بدی is a noun 'badness/harm' and زیان is another noun 'harm/detriment'; the clause is nominal and poetic—the copula ('was') is omitted, so the sense is 'whose badness was a detriment to the Romans.' را marks رومیان as the affected party.
segments.s512-g19: سپردم is first‑person past tense 'I entrusted'. به introduces the indirect object 'to/for', هر 'every', and مهتری کشوری is a nominal phrase 'the chief of the province' (مهتری = chief/elder; کشوری = of the country/provincial). The whole segment means 'I entrusted to each provincial chief.'
segments.s512-g20: که here is a relative marker 'that/which' referring back to what was entrusted. گردد is an archaic/formal present/subjunctive of 'گشتن' meaning 'become' or 'may become'. بر آن پادشاهی سری = 'over that kingdom a (پادشاهی) سری (سری = secret/hidden or 'secret of kingship') — the phrase describes the function or result of what was handed over. The verb is optative/relative, indicating the intended outcome.
segments.s512-g21: همانا is an emphatic adverb 'verily/truly'. نیازش is a noun with possessive clitic meaning 'his need'; نیاید is a negative present/subjunctive 'will not come' or 'shall not arise'. به روم marks the object/goal of that need ('need for Rome'). The clause literally says 'truly, his need for Rome will not arise.'
segments.s512-g22: برآساید is a verb meaning 'be appeased / find ease / be at peace' (3rd person singular, optative/statement). آن کشور و مرز و بوم is the compound subject 'that country and its borders and homeland.' The verb precedes the subject here (poetic inversion), so the meaning is 'that country and its borders and land will be at peace.'
segments.s512-g23: This is an imperative/optative instruction. مرا (here split into م + را in the tokenization) is the object 'me' (را marks it as definite/direct object). مرده 'dead' describes the speaker's state; در خاک مصر 'in the soil of Egypt' gives the location; آگنید is an archaic 2nd‑person plural/formal imperative or optative of آگندن meaning 'cover/fill' (i.e. 'bury/cover with'). The verb coming at the end is normal in classical Persian.
segments.s512-g24: ز here means 'from / with respect to'. گفتار من 'my speech/words' (گفتار = utterance, من = my). هیچ is an emphatic negative 'not at all/none', and مپراگنید is a negative imperative/optative form meaning 'do not corrupt/garble/distort' (the prefix م- conveys the negative in this archaic form and the ending -ید is 2nd person plural/formal). Together: 'Do not in any way corrupt my words.'
segments.s512-g1: به بابل (prepositional phrase) places the action geographically: به + place = 'to/in Babylon' (classical Persian often uses به for location/direction). هم‌ان روز is a poetic form meaning 'that same day' (هم‌ان = emphatic 'the same/very'); روز is the noun 'day'. شد is the past tense of شدن 'to become' and here is the main verb ('became'); دردمند is an adjective 'sorrowful/in pain' describing the subject (understood from context). Word order is typical: adverbials/prepositional phrase + time phrase + verb + predicate.
segments.s512-g2: بدانست is a past tense verb 'he knew/realised' (بد + دانست). کامد is an archaic/past- or imperfect-like form meaning 'would come / was coming' (classical poetic present/preterite sense). به تنگی is a prepositional phrase here indicating source/reason ('by/from hardship' or 'because of distress') and گزند is the noun 'harm/damage' that is expected to arrive; the clause reports his knowledge that harm would come because of the tightness/distress.
segments.s512-g3: دبیر جهاندیده را پیش خواند is a verb phrase: خواند here means 'called/summoned' (پیش خواند = 'had (someone) come before him'). دبیر جهاندیده is the direct object 'the worldly‑wise secretary' and را is the definite/object marker placed after the object. The order Object + را + verb is standard in Persian narrative.
segments.s512-g4: هرانچش به دل بود = 'whatever was in his heart': هرانچش ('all that/whatever') is the indefinite relative phrase; به دل بود is a copular clause ('was in (his) heart') where بود is the past copula. با او براند follows and means 'he related/told it to him'—با او marks the indirect object 'to him'; براند (an old verb form) here functions as 'conveyed/related it' (poetic usage). Overall this segment reports that he communicated everything on his mind to the summoned secretary.
segments.s512-g5: به مادر یکی نامه فرمود و گفت: به + مادر = 'to his mother' (dative); یکی نامه = 'a certain letter' or 'a letter' (یکی = a/certain); فرمود is a formal/archaic verb used for 'he sent/ordered/said'—here it introduces the act of sending the letter; و گفت ('and said') links to the reported speech that follows. Word order: indirect object phrase + object + verb, then coordinating conjunction + verb.
segments.s512-g6: که introduces reported content ('that ...'). آگاهی مرگ = 'the knowledge/awareness of death' (آگاهی = knowledge; مرگ = death; genitive relationship is implicit). نتوان is the negative potential 'cannot' (classical contraction of نتواند), and نهفت is the verbal adjective/infinitive 'to hide' or 'be hidden' from نهفتن; together نتوان نهفت = 'it cannot be hidden'. This clause states the content of what he said in the letter.
segments.s512-g7: ز گیتی مرا بهره این بد که بود is a reflective/gnomic line. ز گیتی = 'from the world' (ز = from, poetic). مرا + را (written here split as م + را) yields 'to me/my' (مرا = me/my share) with را as the object marker; بهره این بد = 'this bad share/lot' (بهره = share; این بد = this ill/evil). که بود is a subordinating 'that it was' introducing the content of the share. Note: the clitic مرا appears split in the printed tokens (م + را) — treat them together as the object pronoun 'me/my'.
segments.s512-g8: زمان چون نکاهد نشاید فزود: زمان = 'time'; چون here means 'since/when' (introducing a causal/conditional clause). نکاهد is an archaic verbal form (ن‑کاهد) meaning 'does not decrease/lessen' (کاهد < کاستن 'to lessen'); نشاید = 'it is not fitting / cannot' (negative modal, classical flavor); فزود is an old/intransitive form meaning 'increase' (from فزودن/فزاییدن). The line expresses a paradoxical thought: if time does not diminish, it cannot increase — a poetic reflection on limits.
segments.s512-g9: The clause addresses the addressee with تو (subject). از مرگ من is a prepositional/locative phrase meaning 'concerning my death' placed before the predicate for emphasis. هیچ before the adjective makes the negation/emphasis ('not at all'). غمگین مشو is a negative imperative (مشو = 'do not become' + adjective) — poetic short form of the negative imperative of شدن.
segments.s512-g10: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause ('for' / 'because'). اندر is the archaic/poetic preposition 'in'. جهان این سخن نیست نو literally orders as 'in the world this saying is not new' — نیست is the copula 'is not' and نو (postpositive adjective) qualifies سخن (here 'saying' / 'thing said'), i.e. 'this is not new in the world.'
segments.s512-g11: هرانکس که is a relative phrase 'whoever/whosoever'. زاید is a poetic/archaic participial/verb form meaning 'is born' (from زادن). ببایدش is an impersonal/modal construction 'it is necessary for him' (lit. 'must be for him'), followed by مرد used here as the verb 'to die' (poetic short form of مردن).
segments.s512-g12: This line gives alternatives: if/whether (اگر) ... or (گر used as an alternative marker). شهریارست‌گر parses as شهریارست + گر: 'one attached to/the worshipper of the king' (a courtier/royalist type). مرد خرد is a noun phrase 'a man of wisdom' where خرد ('wisdom') follows and qualifies مرد ('man'). The whole phrase means 'whether he be a shah‑worshipper/courtier or a wise man.'
segments.s512-g13: بگویم here is the first‑person subjunctive/intentional verb 'I will say / let me say / I shall speak' (poetic use of the verb گفتن). کنون is the adverb 'now' modifying the verb; با بزرگان روم is a prepositional phrase 'with the great men of Rome' (با + plural nominal).
segments.s512-g14: که introduces the subordinate clause. چون here functions temporally 'when/as soon as' (classical/poetic use). بازگردند is the 3rd‑person plural subjunctive/present 'they return'. زین is the archaic/formal contraction of از این ('from this') placed before مرز و بوم ('frontier and homeland'), where مرز و بوم is a fixed pair meaning 'border and native soil.'
segments.s512-g15: نجویند is the 3rd‑person plural present/subjunctive 'they seek' with the negative prefix ن; combined with جز ('except') it produces the meaning 'they seek nothing except...' (a common classical construction). رای و فرمان تو lists the objects sought: 'your counsel/opinion and your command/order' with تو marking possession.
segments.s512-g16: کسی in the scope of the negative verb برنگردد gives 'no one' (classical negative polarity). برنگردد is the negative subjunctive/present 'will not return / will not revert'. ز is the archaic poetic preposition 'from' (like از) and پیمان تو is 'your covenant/treaty/pledge' (ز + noun + possessor).
segments.s512-g25: به سالی is an adverbial phrase meaning 'each year / annually' (به + سالی; به here is the '-ly/ per' use). ز before دینار is the poetic preposition 'from', so ز دینار من = 'from my dinars' (دینار من = 'my dinars'). Word order places the quantity صدهزار after the possessive phrase, producing 'one hundred thousand (from) my dinars.'
segments.s512-g26: ببخشید is the narrative verb meaning 'gave / bestowed' (classical/past-narrative sense of بخشیدن). بر here marks the beneficiary/recipient (toward/to), so بر مردم خیش‌کار = 'to/upon the ploughing people' (i.e. giving to the ploughmen). Note خیش‌کار is a compound meaning 'those who till / ploughmen.'
segments.s512-g27: گر آید = 'if there comes / if happens' (گر = 'if'; آید is the classical present/hortative used for a conditional 'comes/occurs'). یکی روشنک را پسر = 'a son to Roshanak' — روشنک را uses the object/possessive marker را with the mother's name to indicate 'to Roshanak' (i.e., 'a son belonging to Roshanak'). Word order is typical classical Persian: conditional particle + verb + indefinite + possessor + را + noun.
segments.s512-g28: بود here is the copula in the narrative/predictive sense ('would be' / 'will be' / 'was' depending on register); it links the predicate that follows. بی‌گمان 'undoubtedly' is a sentence adverb modifying the whole clause. زنده نام پدر = 'the father's name alive' — نام is best read as 'name/reputation' (i.e., 'the father's fame will be alive').
segments.s512-g29: نباید + که + باشد = an impersonal prohibition/obligation structure meaning 'it should not be / he ought not to be.' باشد is the subjunctive/present infinitival 'be' after نباید. جزو شاه روم = 'a member/part of the king of Rome' (جزو = 'member/part', here 'among the retinue/subjects of the Roman king').
segments.s512-g30: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'for/that (he)...'. او is the third-person pronoun 'he.' تازه گرداند functions as a compound: تازه 'new' + گرداند 'caused/made to be' — together 'make anew / renew.' گرداند is a causative/action verb in the past-narrative form here, so که او تازه گرداند آن مرز و بوم = 'who would renew that border and land.'
segments.s512-g31: وگر is another conditional 'and if / but if.' دختر آید = 'a daughter comes / is born.' به هنگام is the fixed prepositional phrase 'at the time of' (به + هنگام). بوس here appears as a proper name (time/place/person) and is thus not matched by the provided lexical senses for بوس.
segments.s512-g32: به پیوند = 'into union/connection' (به = 'to/into', پیوند = 'bond/link' used here as 'marriage bond' or 'alliance'). با marks the partner/party 'with.' تخمه فیلقوس = 'the stock/offspring (line) of Filqus' — تخمه here = 'offspring / lineage' and فیلقوس is a personal name (the lineage to which she should be joined).
segments.s512-g73: Imperative نگِر opens the clause ('Look!'); it is followed by the purpose marker تا که + subjunctive بینی, giving 'so that you may see'. The prepositional phrase به گرد جهان ('around the world') places the scope of seeing; word order (verb then purpose clause then prepositional phrase) is typical of poetic/classical Persian.
segments.s512-g74: که introduces the content clause ('that ...'); او is the pronoun subject, نیست is the negative copula 'is not', and the phrase از مرگ خسته‌روان functions as a nominal group meaning 'one of the death-weary' (literally 'from the death weary-souls'), i.e. 'among those wearied by death'. The order places the negated verb before the 'از' phrase, a possible classical/poetic construction meaning 'is not one of...'.
segments.s512-g75: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'; نامه is the direct object 'letter'. به مهر here means 'with a seal' (به = 'by/with' + مهر = 'seal'), and اندر آورد (آورد + اندر) is a compound meaning 'brought in / sealed / inserted' in classical Persian. The conjunction و links the next verb بند بفرمود ('he composed/made a stanza').
segments.s512-g76: بفرمود is an honorific past/optative meaning 'he commanded/said'. تا introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that...', بر is the preposition 'on/upon', and ستور is 'steed/horse'. نوند here is a poetic/archaic verbal form meaning 'they mount / set out' (3rd-person plural subjunctive/imperative sense in context), so the whole clause = 'he ordered that they mount the horses.'
segments.s512-g77: ز بابل ('from Babylon') gives the origin; به روم ('to Rome') marks the destination. آورند is a classical past/plural 'they brought' or 'they reported', and آگهی is the object 'news/information'. The verb precedes the object here, a common order in classical Persian poetry.
segments.s512-g78: که introduces the reported content ('that ...'). تیره شد is a two-word predicate 'became dark' or 'grew bleak'; آن فر شاهنشهی is the subject phrase 'that royal/imperial glory' (آن = 'that/the', فر = 'royal glory', شاهنشهی = 'imperial'). The order places the predicate before the full subject, a stylistic option frequent in epic verse.
segments.s512-g57: ز + چیزی: the initial ز here is the poetic preposition 'of/from' governing the noun چیزی. که introduces a relative clause ('that/which') modifying چیزی; آوردم is first-person past 'I brought' and the clause contains the prepositional phrase از هند و چین 'from India and China'. The phrase literally reads 'of the thing(s) that I brought from India and China.'
segments.s512-g58: ز introduces another list of places ('from ...'); توران و ایران و مکران are joined by و. مکران زمین is a tight compound 'the land of Makran' (زمین = 'land'), so the whole segment means 'from Turan and Iran and the land of Makran.'
segments.s512-g59: بدار و ببخش are paired imperatives/requests: بدار (classical imperative 'keep/retain' or 'take') and ببخش ('give away' / 'grant'); آنچ ('that which') is a fused relative 'that which' and افزون بود is the predicate 'was excessive' (افزون = 'more/excess'). The clause says 'keep and give away that which was extra.'
segments.s512-g60: وز here functions as an archaic/prepositional element linking the sentence (classical 'و ز' = 'and/from/for'), followed by اندازه خویش 'its own measure' (اندازه = 'measure/size', خوی + ش = possessive 'خویش') and the verb بیرون بود 'was beyond/outside'. The construction means 'and it was outside/beyond its own measure.'
segments.s512-g61: به تو حاجت آنستم is a classical word order meaning 'I have need of you' (به تو = 'of/for you', حاجت = 'need', آنستم expresses the speaker's state — first‑person is marked in the verb). ای مهربان is a vocative 'O kind one.'
segments.s512-g62: که introduces a subordinate clause expressing the speaker's desire/purpose ('that ...'). بیدار باشی uses the present subjunctive/optative 2nd person ('that you be awake/alert'); و روشن‑روان is coordinated and functions as a parallel predicate/adjective ('and bright‑souled' / 'clear‑minded').
segments.s512-g63: نداری here denotes 'there is no need' / 'you have no need' (classical noun/phrase meaning 'lack, no need'); تن خویش را is the object 'your own body' with را marking definiteness; رنجه is the root meaning 'to trouble/sadden' (used here as an admonition), and بس is an interjection 'enough' or 'stop it'. Together: 'You have no need to trouble your body — enough.'
segments.s512-g64: که introduces the reason/explanation: اندر جهان نیست ('in the world there is not') with جاوید کس 'an eternal person/anyone' as the logical subject of نیست. The negative copula نیست negates existence here: 'for in the world no one is eternal.'
segments.s512-g49: در و بند is a coordinated noun phrase 'door and fastening' (در = door, بند = clasp/fastening). تابوت ما را is a definite object phrase: تابوت (coffin) + possessiveِ من as postposed من (my/our) and then the object marker را; the object marker indicates that the coffin (possessed by us) is the thing acted upon by the verb that follows. به قیر is a prepositional phrase placed before the verb meaning 'with/into pitch' (here instrument/material). Word order is topicalized: object phrase (تابوت ما را) and instrument (به قیر) come before the verb which will govern them.
segments.s512-g50: بگیرند is a third-person plural verb form (present/subjunctive/optative in classical style) from گرفتن, here meaning 'they take/they smother/they apply' and governs the prior object (تابوت ما را به قیر). The conjunction و connects the verb clause to the following list of aromatic substances (کافور، مشک، عبیر), which are understood as additional materials placed/used — the verb is not repeated for them.
segments.s512-g51: نخست is an adverb 'first/firstly'. آگنند is a third-person plural form (present/subjunctive/epic narrative) of آگندن/آگندن meaning 'to fill/soak/impregnate (with something)' and takes the locative phrase اندرو انگبین as its complement: اندرو = اندر + رو 'inside/within', انگبین = 'nectar/ambrosia' — together 'they fill/soak it inside with nectar.'
segments.s512-g52: زبر انگبین is a noun phrase where زبر (here 'upper/above, top layer') qualifies انگبین ('nectar'), yielding 'a top/upper layer of nectar'. زیر دیبای چین is a locative phrase 'under the Chinese brocade' (زیر = under, دیبای = brocade/silk, چین = China/Chinese), so the clause says they place/put that (nectar) beneath the Chinese silk. Word order places the qualifier زبر before انگبین and the locative زیر ... after it.
segments.s512-g53: ازان پس is a temporal adverbial 'after that/thereupon'. تن من places the noun 'body' before the possessor pronoun من (a common poetic order), yielding 'my body'. نهند is the third-person plural present/subjunctive of نهاد/نهادن here meaning 'they place/they put', and اندران ('among them' / 'therein') gives the location. So: 'After that they put my body among them.'
segments.s512-g54: سرآمد سخن is a nominal phrase 'the end/conclusion of the speech' (سرآمد = conclusion; سخن = speech/words). چون here functions as a clause introducer 'when/as', and برآمد is a past/simple perfective sense 'arose/issued forth' used with روان ('soul/spirit') to mean 'when the spirit departed/issued forth'. The clause thus reads 'at the conclusion of the words, when the spirit departed.'
segments.s512-g55: تو is the vocative/subject 'you'. پند من is a nominal phrase 'my counsel/advice' with the possessor من following the noun (poetic word order). ای introduces a vocative 'O', and مادر پرخرد is the addressee 'mother, very wise' (پرخرد = 'very wise'). The whole is an address: 'You are my counsel, O very-wise mother.'
segments.s512-g56: نگه‌دار here is an imperative/hortative 'keep watch/protect' (addressing the mother). تا introduces a subordinate temporal clause 'until'; روز من 'my day' = 'my time/life' (روز = day/time, followed by possessor من), and بگذرد is a present-subjunctive/optative verb 'passes' used after تا to indicate the event to be awaited. Together: 'Keep watch until my day passes.'
segments.s512-g65: روانم (roân-am) = 'my soul' (noun + 1st‑person suffix). روان ترا = روان + ترا (ت = 2nd‑person suffix 'you' + را = object marker), i.e. 'your soul' as the object. بی‌گمان is an adverb 'undoubtedly' placed after the object for emphasis; the whole chunk supplies subject + object + adverb before the verb.
segments.s512-g66: ببیند is 3rd‑person present/subjunctive form of 'دیدن' with the -be‑ prefix often carrying potential/future sense here ('will see'). چو = 'when' introduces the temporal clause. تنگ modifies زمان (= 'tight/urgent'); اندر آید زمان literally 'time comes in' with زمان (time/moment) as the subject at the end — overall: 'when the time comes tight/urgent.'
segments.s512-g67: شکیبایی is the noun 'patience' (subject). از مهر = 'than love' (comparative marker از with the noun مهر). نامی‌تر است is the comparative predicate 'is more famed/notable' (adjective نامی 'famous' with comparative -تر plus copula است). The order is X از Y نامی‌تر است = 'X is more famous than Y.'
segments.s512-g68: This clause states a generalization by inversion: سبکسر بود = 'was/is rash' (بود here functions as the copula). هرک = 'whoever' (indefinite relative), او = 'he' (subject pronoun), کهتر است = 'is lesser/inferior' (adjective کهتر + است). Put together: 'Whoever is rash is the lesser one.'
segments.s512-g69: ترا = ت (2nd‑person suffix) + را (object marker) = 'you'/'your' as object; مهر بد = 'ill/bad love' (noun + adjective). بر تنم = 'upon my body' (preposition بر + تنم 'my body'). سال و ماه = duration 'years and months'. The verb is omitted here (optative/wish construction); the phrase functions as a jussive blessing/curse: 'May your bad love be upon my body for years and months.'
segments.s512-g70: کنون = 'now' (adverb). جان پاکم = 'my pure soul' (جان 'soul' + پاکم functioning adjectivally/possessively 'my pure'). ز یزدان = 'from/of God' (z = from, یزدان = God). بخواه is the 2nd‑person imperative of خواستن 'ask (for)' so the clause means 'Now, ask of God my pure soul.'
segments.s512-g71: بدین خواستن = 'in/by this asking' (بدین is the classical contraction of به این, and خواستن is used as a verbal noun 'asking/request'). باش is the 2nd‑person imperative 'be'. فریادرس is a predicate noun 'a rescuer/succourer.' The whole imperative: 'In this asking, be a rescuer.'
segments.s512-g72: که introduces the result/purpose clause 'so that'. فریادرس (again) 'rescuer'. باشدم is a 1st‑person optative/subjunctive form 'may I be' (classical ending -دم here expresses the speaker's wish). دست‌رس is a predicative adjective 'at hand/available'. So: 'so that I may be a rescuer at hand.'

sentences.s512-s21: When he became of one mind to go, they sent someone with him to India.
sentences.s512-s22: I have done every task here; in my misery I set my heart on death.
sentences.s512-s23: First, let them make a golden coffin; let them wrap my body in a shroud perfumed with ambergris.
sentences.s512-s24: Who is worthy to wrap me in Chinese gold-brocade? Who will wrap (me) out of my solicitude?
sentences.s512-s17: You are his adopted son, not my son‑in‑law; make my remembrance fresh for him in the world.
sentences.s512-s18: Send the other daughter, Kēd, unharmed to her venerable father.
sentences.s512-s19: Aba, Yāreh, a slave, and a well‑wisher — Ammar mustered them and set out with him on the road.
sentences.s512-s20: The same helmet and jewel and silver and gold which he had brought from his father before.
sentences.s512-s9: Whoever was among the Iranians whose evil harmed the Romans.
sentences.s512-s10: I entrusted to every provincial chief that which would become (or serve as) a secret kingship over that realm.
sentences.s512-s11: Truly, he will have no need of Rome; that country, its borders and homeland will be at peace.
sentences.s512-s12: Bury me dead in the soil of Egypt; from my words do not at all corrupt/distort (anything).
sentences.s512-s1: That very day in Babylon he became sorrowful; he knew that harm would come from this hardship.
sentences.s512-s2: He summoned the worldly‑wise secretary and told him whatever was in his heart.
sentences.s512-s3: He sent a letter to his mother and said that one cannot hide the knowledge of death.
sentences.s512-s4: From the world my lot was this ill thing: that when time does not lessen, it cannot increase.
sentences.s512-s5: Do not be at all sorrowful about my death, for in the world this saying is not new.
sentences.s512-s6: Whoever is born must die; whether he be a courtier of the king or a man of wisdom.
sentences.s512-s7: I will now speak with the great men of Rome, that when they return from this frontier and native soil...
sentences.s512-s8: They will seek nothing but your counsel and command; no one will break away from your covenant.
sentences.s512-s13: Each year he gave away one hundred thousand of my dinars to the ploughing people.
sentences.s512-s14: If a son comes to Roshanak, surely the father's name will live on.
sentences.s512-s15: He ought not to be among the Roman king's men, for he would make that border and land new.
sentences.s512-s16: And if a daughter is born at the time of Bus, let her be joined in marriage with the house/line of Filqus.
sentences.s512-s37: Look, so that you may see around the world that he is not among those whose souls are wearied by death.
sentences.s512-s38: When he sealed the letter and composed a stanza, he commanded that they mount the horses.
sentences.s512-s39: They brought news from Babylon to Rome that the imperial glory had turned dark.
sentences.s512-s29: Of the things I brought from India and China, and from Turan and Iran and the land of Makran.
sentences.s512-s30: Keep (hold) and give away that which was excessive, for it lay beyond its proper measure.
sentences.s512-s31: I need you, O kind one, that you be awake and quick‑witted (bright‑souled).
sentences.s512-s32: Do not distress your body — enough of that — for there is no one in the world who is eternal.
sentences.s512-s25: They seal the door and fastenings of our coffin with pitch, and (fill it with) camphor, musk, and ambergris.
sentences.s512-s26: First they anoint (or fill) it inside with nectar/ambrosia, then a top layer of nectar beneath the Chinese brocade.
sentences.s512-s27: After that they lay my body among them; at the close of speech, when the spirit departed.
sentences.s512-s28: You are my counsel, O very-wise mother; keep watch until my day (my life) passes.
sentences.s512-s33: My soul will surely see your soul when the time grows tight.
sentences.s512-s34: Patience is more renowned than love; whoever is rash is the inferior one.
sentences.s512-s35: May your ill love lie upon my body for years and months; now ask of God my pure soul.
sentences.s512-s36: In this request be a succourer, so that I may be a rescuer at hand.
